coredata.dc:title: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots delay postharvest senescence through repressing BrTCP9-mediated reactive oxygen species metabolism in flowering Chinese cabbage

coredata.dc:description: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs), as a novel type of nanomaterials, play an important role in delaying senescence and maintaining quality in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. However, understanding the underlying molecular mechanisms of SmCDs involved in delaying senescence is still unclear and requires further studies. In this work, the expression of BrTCP9 among the 16 BrTCPs members was found to correlate with the morphological phenotype observed during leaf aging. Silencing BrTCP9 hindered the degradation of chlorophyll, inhibited the expressions of chlorophyll decomposition-associated genes (BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH) and respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRbohA and BrRbohD), increased the activities of peroxidase (POD) and superoxide dismutase (SOD), slowed down the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and enhanced Fv/Fm value, which ultimately delayed leaf senescence. Interestingly, the changing trends of chlorophyll content, chlorophyll degradation-related gene expression, ROS production, Fv/Fm value, and leaf phenotype after silencing BrRbohA were similar to those of silencing BrTCP9. Notably, SmCDs could downregulate the transcriptions of BrTCP9 and BrRbohA, attenuate the production of ROS, and delay leaf senescence. Furthermore, BrTCP9 could directly bind to the GGTCCCA motif in the promoter region of BrRbohA and activate its transcript. Our findings demonstrated that BrTCP9 served as a positive regulator of leaf senescence in flowering Chinese cabbage. In contrast, SmCDs effectively delayed leaf senescence by suppressing BrTCP9-mediated ROS metabolism to reduce the accumulation of ROS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Expression levels of TCP family members in flowering Chinese cabbage under SmCDs treatment during storage. The vertical bars represent the standard deviation of the mean (n = 3). Asterisks above columns display statistically significant differences at P < 0.05.

Molecular characterization of BrTCP9. (A) Phenotypes of different parts of senescent leaves at 14 d of storage. B is the basal part, M is the middle part, and T is the top part. (B) The expression level of BrTCP9 in different parts (B, M, and T) of senescent leaves. (C) Phylogenetic tree construction of BrTCP9 with multiple TCP family members in Arabidopsis thaliana. (D) Multiple sequence comparison of the amino acid sequences of AtTCP9 and BrTCP9. Red boxes represent conserved bHLH structural domains. (E) Subcellular localization of BrTCP9 is demonstrated in tobacco leaves. 35S::GFP is the negative control. Bars= 25 μm. NLS: nuclear localization signal. (F) Transcriptional activation activity of BrWRKY12 in tobacco. Each value represents the mean ± SD of six biological repetitions. The asterisks above columns represent statistically significant differences at P < 0.05.

The BrTCP9 function in the senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage under the influence of SmCDs. (A) Senescent phenotype of isolated leaves after 5 d of dark induction. (B) Total chlorophyll content in isolated leaves after dark induction for 5 d. (C) Chlorophyll fluorescence imaging. (D) The maximum fluorescence Fv/Fm values of isolated leaves after 5 d of dark induction. (E) Expression levels of chlorophyll degradation genes in isolated leaves after 5 d of dark induction. The vertical bars show the standard deviation of the mean (n = 3). Letters above columns represent statistically significant differences at P < 0.05.

The function assay of BrTCP9 in ROS metabolism of flowering Chinese cabbage. (A) DAB and NBT staining of silenced BrTCP9 leaves at various periods after dark stress induction. (B) Cell membrane permeability in leaves of silenced BrTCP9 plants at various periods after dark stress induction. (C) SOD activity. (D) POD activity. (E) CAT activity. (F) Expression levels of BrRboh genes within leaves of BrTCP9-silenced plants after dark stress induction. SmCDs: 1 mg·mL−1 Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. The vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). Letters and asterisks above columns represent statistically significant differences at P < 0.05.

BrTCP9 acts directly on the promoter region of BrRbohA and activates its expression. (A) Cis-acting element analysis of the BrRbohA promoter region (2000 bp upstream of ATG). (B) In vivo fluorescence imaging of plants; (C) LUC/REN enzyme activity. 62SK is used as an empty vector control. Values indicate the mean ± SD of six biological repetitions. The asterisks above the columns display a significant difference at P < 0.05. (D) Y1H experiment verified that BrTCP9 binds to cis-acting elements in the BrRbohA promoter region. (E) GUS tissue staining experiment demonstrated that BrTCP9 activates BrRbohA expression. Tobacco leaf phenotypes were taken on 3 d after infestation. At least three independent infestation experiments were performed. (F) EMSA experiment verified BrTCP9 binds to the BrRbohA promoter region. Biotin-probe was a biotin-labelled probe for the BrRbohA promoter, with the labelled sequence GGTCCCAC highlighted in red font; cold-probe was an unlabelled competing probe; mutant-probe was a biotin-labelled probe with the BrRbohA promoter sequence partially mutated out. GST-labelled BrTCP9 (BrTCP9-GST) was purified and used for DNA binding assays.

The function assay of BrRBohA in leaf senescence under the impact of SmCDs. (A) Senescent phenotypes of BrRBohA-silenced isolated leaves after 5 d of darkness. (B) Total chlorophyll content of silenced BrRBohA leaves after 5 d of darkness. (C) Chlorophyll fluorescence imaging. (D) Maximum fluorescence Fv/Fm values. (E) NBT and DAB tissue staining of BrRBohA-silenced isolated leaves after 5 d of darkness. (F) Expression levels of chlorophyll degradation genes in BrRBohA-silenced isolated leaves after 5 d of dark induction. SmCDs: 1 mg·mL−1 Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3), and different letters denote significance of the differences (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots delay postharvest senescence through repressing BrTCP9-mediated reactive oxygen species metabolism in flowering Chinese cabbage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs), as a novel type of nanomaterials, play an important role in delaying senescence and maintaining quality in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. However, understanding the underlying molecular mechanisms of SmCDs involved in delaying senescence is still unclear and requires further studies. In this work, the expression of BrTCP9 among the 16 BrTCPs members was found to correlate with the morphological phenotype observed during leaf aging. Silencing BrTCP9 hindered the degradation of chlorophyll, inhibited the expressions of chlorophyll decomposition-associated genes (BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH) and respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRbohA and BrRbohD), increased the activities of peroxidase (POD) and superoxide dismutase (SOD), slowed down the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and enhanced Fv/Fm value, which ultimately delayed leaf senescence. Interestingly, the changing trends of chlorophyll content, chlorophyll degradation-related gene expression, ROS production, Fv/Fm value, and leaf phenotype after silencing BrRbohA were similar to those of silencing BrTCP9. Notably, SmCDs could downregulate the transcriptions of BrTCP9 and BrRbohA, attenuate the production of ROS, and delay leaf senescence. Furthermore, BrTCP9 could directly bind to the GGTCCCA motif in the promoter region of BrRbohA and activate its transcript. Our findings demonstrated that BrTCP9 served as a positive regulator of leaf senescence in flowering Chinese cabbage. In contrast, SmCDs effectively delayed leaf senescence by suppressing BrTCP9-mediated ROS metabolism to reduce the accumulation of ROS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Flowering Chinese cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee) is a leafy vegetable belonging to the Cruciferae family. It is rich in various nutrients but it is prone to rapid aging and nutrient degradation after harvest (Yue et al., 2022, 2023). Previous researches have ascertained that the various chemical treatments, including 1-methylcyclopropene, benzoic acid, chlorine dioxide, and melatonin, can procrastinate the senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage (Kuang et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2022, 2023). However, these treatments pose potential risks to the food safety of agricultural products. As a result of that, it is urgent to develop environmentally friendly and safe preservation materials and technologies for postharvest agricultural products. Carbon dots (CDs), as a burgeoning nanomaterial, have obvious advantages in promoting plant growth and improving plant tolerance under various stress conditions because of their small size, excellent chemical and photochemical stabilities, good water solubility and biocompatibility, and strong antioxidant capacity (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs) are synthesized using biomass Salvia miltiorrhiza as the precursor and found to have a good scavenging effect on intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in plant (Li et al., 2021; Kuang et al., 2023). Our recent innovative findings have confirmed that SmCDs can effectively delay postharvest senescence and maintain the quality of flowering Chinese cabbage (Kuang et al., 2023). However, the molecular mechanisms through which SmCDs regulate postharvest senescence remain largely unexplored and require further elucidation.

Studies have testified that many transcription factors play crucial roles in the leaf senescence process, including NAC, WRKY, MYB, and TCP (Kim et al., 2016). As a plant-specific class of transcription factors, TCP has important roles in plant growth, organ development, and senescence process (Balsemão-Pires et al., 2013). TCP comprises a conserved DNA-binding structural domain, known as the TCP structural domain, which contains a 59-amino acid basic helix-loop-helix (bHLH) structure (Martín-Trillo and Cubas, 2010). Previous work showed that CmTCP14 of chrysanthemum inhibits Arabidopsis thaliana organ development and delays the onset of senescence when heterologously expressed in Arabidopsis thaliana plants (Zhang et al., 2017). TCP2 regulates leaf morphogenesis, impacts flowering, and expedites leaf senescence in Arabidopsis thaliana, and this regulatory effect is achieved in part by affecting the transcription levels of Jasmonic acid (JA) biosynthetic genes, including AtLOX2, AtLOX3, and AtLOX4 (Zhu et al., 2021). BrTCP7 promotes leaf aging by activating the transcription of JA biosynthesis gene BrOPR3 and chlorophyll degradation gene BrRCCR in flowering Chinese cabbage (Xu et al., 2019). BrTCP21 can bind to the promoter region of GA biosynthesis gene BrGA20ox3 and activate its transcription, thus promoting GA biosynthesis and delaying leaf senescence (Xiao et al., 2019). To date, TCP transcription factors mainly regulate the aging process through phytohormone pathway, and whether TCPs can regulate the plant senescence through other biological pathways needs to be further explored.

Plant senescence is commonly regarded as a process of programmed cell death, impacted by a multitude of external and internal factors, and significantly affects both the yield and quality of crops (Woo et al., 2013). Previous reports demonstrated that plant senescence is closely related to chlorophyll decomposition, ethylene and ROS production (Tan et al., 2020, 2021). ROS is a by-product of aerobic metabolism in plants, which mainly includes hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion radical (O2 •-) and hydroxyl radical (OH•). ROS mainly produced by respiratory burst oxidase homologue (Rboh, also known as NADPH oxidase), peroxidases, and amine oxidases, can be used as a second messenger to regulate plant development, biotic and abiotic stress of plants (Foreman et al., 2003). It is well established that the onset of leaf aging is closely related to the regulation of ROS homeostasis (Fu et al., 2019). Previous researches have demonstrated that Rboh genes are involved in modulating plant senescence and regulated by WRKY and NAC transcription factors (Fan et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2020). Recent findings revealed that WRKY transcription factor BnaWSR1ca and NAC transcription factor BnaNTL1 activate BnaRbohD transcription, respectively, and promote ROS production and cell death, thereby inducing leaf senescence (Cui et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021). BrNAC055 directly binds to the BrRbohB and BrRbohC-like promoters and activates their expression, which lead to increasing ROS levels and chlorophyll degradation and accelerating leaf senescence in flowering Chinese cabbage (Fan et al., 2018). BnaWGR1 transcription factor in Brassica napus binds to the promoters of BnaRbohD and BnaRbohF and activates their transcript, respectively, resulting in the accumulation of ROS and leaf senescence (Yang et al., 2018). However, the involvement of TCP family members in mediating ROS metabolism to regulate leaf senescence has not been previously reported. Our earlier studies have demonstrated that SmCDs exhibit remarkable scavenging efficiencies for DPPH, O2 •-, and OH• radicals, achieving rates of 88.9 %, 95.6 %, and 71.4 %, respectively, and play a crucial role in modulating the transcript of Rboh genes in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage (Li et al., 2021; Kuang et al., 2023). Although Rboh genes are closely associated with ROS production and TCPs are key transcription factor in regulating senescence, the molecular mechanisms through which TCPs interact with Rboh genes to influence senescence under the impacts of SmCDs remain largely unexplored.

This study aims to elucidate the roles of the BrTCP9 transcription factor and the respiratory burst oxidase homologue gene (RbohA) in regulating postharvest senescence in flowering Chinese cabbage subjected to the impact of SmCDs. Our findings shed light on a novel transcriptional regulatory mechanism involving the BrTCP9-BrRbohA module, which contributes to delaying postharvest senescence. These insights have significant implications for the development of advanced postharvest handling material and technology designed to prolong shelf life, preserve quality, and enhance the commercial value of horticultural products.

SmCDs were synthesized according to our previously reported method (Wang et al., 2021). Briefly, Salvia miltiorrhiza powders (15 g) were dissolved in double-distilled water (300 mL) and subjected to ultrasonic treatment (Puluody, Shanghai, China) for 30 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 500 mL Teflon-lined autoclave (Chemours, Wilmington, USA) and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The solution was then centrifuged at 8500 g for 20 min. The supernatant liquid was collected and filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane (Zinteng, Tianjin, China). Small molecules in the solution were removed using a 1000 Da cellulose dialysis bag (MesGen Biotech, Shanghai, China). The final product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chlorophyll content and maximal fluorescence (Fv/ Fm) value
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorophyll content of leaves was assessed using the acetone-ethanol mixture method (Arkus et al., 2005). Briefly, fresh leaves (0.5 g) were submerged in a mixture of ethanol and acetone (v: v=1: 1) under dark conditions until the leaves were completely decoloured. Finally, the absorbance values of the mixture were determined by a Uv-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Guangzhou, China) under OD645 and OD663 conditions. Fv/Fm value was evaluated according to the previous report (Behr et al., 2010) using the IMAGING-PAM chlorophyll fluorometer system (WALZ, Nuremberg, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s specification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diaminobenzidine (DAB) and nitrogen blue tetrazolium (NBT) histochemical staining
ce:section.ce:para: The accumulation of ROS was shown by DAB and NBT tissue staining, which was carried out following the previous method (Liu et al., 2020). DAB solution was prepared with 50 mM Tris-HCL, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 3.8. NBT solution was prepared with 25 mM HEPES buffer, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.8. The leaves were then submerged in the configured DAB and NBT staining solutions for 6–8 h in the dark. Finally, the leaves were decoloured with 95 % ethanol in a boiling water bath until the chlorophyll was completely faded and ready for photographic retention.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total RNA extraction, cDNA preparation and real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR (qRT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A TRIzol kit (Magen, Guangzhou, China) was used for total RNA extraction in all experiments. Briefly, 0.3 g of flowering Chinese cabbage leaves were weighed and thoroughly ground into powder. Then, 1 mL of TRIzon Reagent was promptly added for complete lysis. The RNA was subsequently purified following the manufacturer’s specification and stored at −80 °C. First-strand cDNA was synthesized from 1 μg of total RNA using the PrimeScript™ RT Master Mix kit (Takara, Dalian, China), with the resulting cDNA stored at −20 °C for further use. qPCR was carried out using Q711-ChamQTM Universal SYBR® qPCR Master Mix (Vazyme, Nanjing, China) and a Bio-Rad CFX96 Real-Time PCR system (Bio-Rad, Hercules, USA). Primers were synthesized by Bio-engineering (Sangon Biotech, Shanghai, China), with actin serving as the internal control gene. The specific sequences of the gene-specific primers are listed in Table S1. Transcript levels were evaluated using the 2-ΔΔCt method (Pfaffl, 2001).

Silencing fragments for BrTCP9 and BrRbohA were predicted using the VIGS tool available on the Solanaceae Genomics Network website. Primers containing EcoRI and BamHI restriction sites were designed for PCR amplification of these fragments, with the specific primer sequences detailed in Table S1. The silencing fragments were then cloned into the TRV vector, resulting in recombinant plasmids TRV-BrTCP9, TRV-BrRbohA, and TRV2 (empty vector), which were subsequently transformed into Agrobacterium tumefaciens GV3101. Experimental procedures were conducted following our previous study (Yue et al., 2023). Infected flowering Chinese cabbage plants were cultured in the dark for 3 d in an incubator with artificial light at 23 ± 2 °C, followed by a shift to a normal photoperiod (16 h light/8 h dark). qPCR was periodically performed on the leaves, and plants with high silencing efficiency were selected for further validation analyses.

The CDS sequence of BrTCP9 was amplified by specific primers and constructed on the pCAMBIA1300 vector. Agrobacterium tumefaciens was co-transformed with the recombinant plasmid 35S: BrTCP9-GFP or the control plasmid 35S: GFP as well as the nuclear marker NLS-RFP (NLS, nuclear localisation sequence). The cultured and reselected Agrobacterium was then injected into 4–6-week-old tobacco (Nicotiana benthamiana) leaves using a 1 mL syringe without a needle. After 48 h of infiltration, GFP fluorescence signals in the cells were observed using an Axioskop 2 Plus fluorescence microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany).

The coding sequence of BrTCP9 was amplified by specific primers and seamlessly cloned into the pGADT7 vector. Similarly, the promoter fragment of BrRbohA (2000 bp upstream of the predicted translation initiation site) was amplified and ligated into the pAbAi vector as a bait. The pAbAi-RbohA recombinant plasmid was linearized and then transformed into Y1H yeast cells according to the instructions of the Yeastmaker Yeast Transformation System 2 (Takara, Dalian, China), and cultured for about 3 d in a defective medium of SD/Ura. Yeast receptor cells containing the BrRbohA promoter fragment are then prepared following the manufacturer's instruction, and BrTCP9 is co-transformed into the receptor cells and cultured further in SD/-Leu medium. Finally, single colonies were picked and dissolved in 0.9 % NaCl solution, and the bacterial solution was uniformly adjusted to OD600=0.003, and dropped on SD/-Leu (containing AbA antibiotic) solid medium to observe the growth status.

The full-length CDS sequence of BrTCP9 was amplified by specific primers and fused to the pGreenII 62-SK vector as an effector. The promoter fragment of BrRbohA (2000 bp upstream of ATG) was constructed into pGreenII 0800-LUC vector as a reporter. The constructed recombinant plasmids were transformed into Agrobacterium competent cells separately, and the Agrobacterium infestation solution containing effector and reporter (Veffector: Vreporter = 9:1) was injected into tobacco leaves. Forty-eight hours after infiltration, LUC and REN values were read on a Luminoskan Ascent microplate luminescence instrument (Thermo, Massachusetts, USA) following the specification of the LUC Assay Kit (Promega, Wisconsin, USA). The values of LUC/REN were used to reflect the effector and reporter of BrTCP9 on the regulation of PGreenII 62-SK (empty vector).

Plant in vivo fluorescence imaging of infested tobacco leaves was performed using a Berthold/NightSHADE LB985 (Berthold, Nanjing, China) instrument. D-fluorescein potassium salt (Beyotime, Guangzhou, China) was uniformly applied on the back of the leaves before photographing and incubated at 37 °C for more than 5 min, followed by immediate imaging analysis.

The coding sequence of BrTCP9 was recombined into the pRI101 vector as an effector using specific primers, according to the vector construction method described in our previous study (Yue et al., 2022). The promoter fragment of BrRbohA (2000 bp upstream of the translation initiation site) was cloned into the pBI101 vector to serve as a reporter gene. GUS tissue staining assays, including destaining and photography, were conducted according to the method reported by Ma et al. (2021).

EMSA reaction experiments were carried out according to the previous method (Cai et al., 2022). The full-length coding sequence of BrTCP9 containing the DNA-binding domain was amplified and inserted into the pGEX-4T vector at the EcoRI and XhoI digestion sites. The constructed recombinant plasmid was transformed into BL21-CodonPlus (DE3)-RIL receptor cells (Wei Di, Shanghai, China), and protein expression was induced with 1 M isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactoside (IPTG) at 37 ℃ 200 rpm. The soluble recombinant GST-BrTCP9 protein was purified according to the instructions of GSTSep Glutathione MagBeads kit (Yeasen, Shanghai, China). The probe was derived from the BrRbohA promoter region containing the TCP-binding sequence, synthesized and labelled with biotin by Beijing SBS Genetech Co., Ltd.

All data obtained in the experiments were analysed by SPSS software and were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) of at least three independent experiment replicates. P-value of < 0.05 or 0.01 represented a significant statistical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression patterns of BrTCP transcription factors involved in regulating postharvest senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage under SmCDs treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous study demonstrated that SmCDs are effective in delaying the leaf senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage (Kuang et al., 2023). To further clarify the molecular mechanism of SmCDs involved in procrastinating postharvest senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage, the expression patterns of BrTCP transcription factors in leaves treated with SmCDs were first analyzed using qRT-PCR, based on data from the Brassica rapa genome database. The results indicated that the expressions of 16 BrTCP transcription factors showed different trends during storage of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage ( Fig. 1). Among the BrTCP transcription factors analyzed, the expression levels of BrTCP9 and BrTCP20 were up-regulated during storage but were inhibited by SmCDs. Other BrTCP transcription factors did not exhibit consistent changes under SmCDs treatment during storage. Given the detailed studies on TCP20 (Danisman et al., 2013), the focus was directed towards investigating the potential role of BrTCP9 in regulating postharvest senescence in response to SmCDs treatment. It was observed that the transcript levels of BrTCP9 were reduced by 70 %, 10 %, and 54 % in SmCDs-treated samples compared to controls at 7, 14, and 21 d of storage, respectively (Fig. 1). Interestingly, the expression of BrTCP9 was similar with that of senescence marker gene (BrSAG12), chlorophyll degradation-associated genes (BrNYE1, BrNOL, BrNYC1, BrPAO, and BrPPH), and respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRbohA, BrRbohB, and BrRbohD) in our previously study during storage (Kuang et al., 2023). It can be speculated that SmCDs delaying senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage was closely related to the inhibition of BrTCP9 transcription. Based on these findings, we preliminarily confirmed BrTCP9 as the potential key regulatory factor responsible for regulating postharvest senescence mediated ROS metabolism under the influence of SmCDs treatment, which warrants further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular characterization of BrTCP9 transcription factor
ce:section.ce:para: To gain further insights into the response of BrTCP9 to senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage, representative leaf from 14 d of storage was divided into three segments (base, middle, and top parts) to analyze the BrTCP9 spatiotemporal expression pattern of different aging degrees ( Fig. 2A). The results displayed that the transcript level of BrTCP9 was higher in top part with high senescence degree (obvious yellowing) than that in base part with low senescence degree (low yellowing) (Fig. 2B), suggesting that BrTCP9 might positively regulate leaf senescence. Additionally, phylogenetic analysis showed that BrTCP9 was the most homologous with and closely related to Arabidopsis thaliana AtTCP9 (Fig. 2C). Multiple sequence alignment revealed that BrTCP9 was found to contain a typical TCP structural domain and a basic helix-loop-helix (bHLH) motif of 60 amino acid residues (Fig. 2D), which is a very important site for binding to target genes. The coding sequence length of BrTCP9 is 936 bp, with 311 amino acids, 33.17 kDa molecular weight, and 10.28 isoelectric point (Table S2). In order to understand the location of BrTCP9 in the cell, a BrTCP9-GFP fusion protein was constructed and subsequently transformed into tobacco leaves for transient expression. As depicted in Fig. 2E, the GFP fluorescence signal of the positive control was detected in the nucleus and the cell membrane, while the fluorescence signal of BrTCP9-GFP was only observed in the nucleus, which proved that the BrTCP9 transcription factor is located in the nucleus. The transcriptional activation activity of BrTCP9 was then further explored by LUC assay. As shown in Fig. 2F, pBD-BrTCP9 and the positive control VP16 substantially increased the LUC/REN ratio compared to the pBD negative control, indicating that the BrTCP9 transcription factor exerted a transcriptional activation function on downstream target genes. Taken together, BrTCP9 was a nuclear-localized transcriptional activator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BrTCP9 positively regulates leaf senescence and is suppressed by SmCDs in flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: Leaf yellowing is a typical phenomenon of plant senescence, and dark treatment of isolated leaves is a typical method to study the senescence of plants. To better expound the functional roles of BrTCP9 involved in regulating senescence, transgenic flowering Chinese cabbage lines containing TRV-BrTCP9 were constructed using VIGS technology (Fig. S1). After 5 d of dark treatment of isolated leaves, TRV2 leaves exhibited completely yellowing symptoms, while those of TRV-BrTCP9 displayed less yellowing phenotype compared with TRV2 leaves ( Fig. 3A). Surprisingly, SmCDs treatment partially alleviated the yellowing phenomenon of TRV2 and TRV-BrTCP9 leaves. Notably, the leaves kept greener in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs than those in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves (Fig. 3A). The chlorophyll content of TRV-BrTCP9 leaves increased by 360.52 % compared to TRV2 plants, while that of TRV2 and TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs increased by 62.85 % and 384.20 %, respectively, which was consistent with the morphological phenotypes (Fig. 3B).

The Fv/Fm represented the maximum photochemical quantum yield of PSII within the photosynthetic system, reflecting both the photosynthetic activity and senescence characteristics of leaves. As depicted in Fig. 3C, after 5 d of darkness, the chlorophyll fluorescence of TRV2 leaves exhibited a large area of orange coloration. In contrast, TRV-BrTCP9 leaves and TRV2 leaves treated with SmCDs displayed a reduced orange coloration. Notably, TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs exhibited significantly less orange coloration compared to untreated TRV-BrTCP9 leaves. The Fv/Fm ratios in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves and TRV2 leaves treated with SmCDs were higher than those observed in TRV2 leaves. Among all treatments, the highest Fv/Fm ratio was recorded in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs (Fig. 3D). After 5 d of dark treatment, the Fv/Fm ratio of TRV-BrTCP9 leaves increased by 84.81 %, while that of TRV2 and TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs increased by 53.81 % and 153.62 %. respectively, which was consistent with the findings of fluorescence imaging (Fig. 3D).

Additionally, silencing BrTCP9 and spraying SmCDs could downregulate the transcripts of four chlorophyll degradation-related genes including BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH. The expression levels of BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves were significantly lower than those of TRV2 leaves, and those in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs were significantly lower than that of TRV-BrTCP9 leaves (Fig. 3E). These results fully demonstrated that silencing BrTCP9 could delay leaf senescence, while this regulation function was further enhanced by SmCDs treatment.

Collectively, these findings provided evidence that BrTCP9 played a positive regulatory role in the leaf senescence process of flowering Chinese cabbage, and SmCDs could inhibit the function of BrTCP9 and delay the leaf senescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibiting TCP9-mediated ROS metabolism is essential for delaying leaf senescence caused by SmCDs in flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous report demonstrated that SmCDs can improve the activities of antioxidant enzyme (SOD, CAT, POD, and APX), repress the transcripts of respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRbohA, BrRbohB, BrRbohD, and BrRbohF), and lower the membrane lipid peroxidation (Kuang et al., 2023). Thus, it is speculated that ROS metabolism might play a crucial role in regulating aging through SmCDs-mediated BrTCP9. To test this hypothesis, TRV-BrTCP9 transgenic plants were generated using VIGS technology (Fig. S1). The identification of H2O2 accumulation was achieved by DAB staining, and the superoxide anion O₂•⁻ content was identified by NBT staining. As shown in Fig. 4A, the NBT and DAB staining results of TRV2 displayed a relatively dark blue and brown color in leaves, respectively, while silencing BrTCP9 and spraying SmCDs had weaker blue NBT and lighter brown DAB staining. Notably, TRV-BrTCP9 leaves treated with SmCDs exhibited weaker blue NBT staining and lighter brown DAB staining compared to untreated TRV-BrTCP9 leaves (Fig. 4A). These findings showed that silencing BrTCP9 combined with SmCDs application effectively reduced the accumulation of H₂O₂ and O₂•⁻, respectively.

The cell membrane permeability of leaves in both TRV2 and BrTCP9-silenced plants increased progressively with extended dark stress treatment. Notably, the permeability in TRV2 plants was significantly higher than in TRV-BrTCP9 plants. However, the application of SmCDs effectively inhibited the increase in permeability. After 5 d of dark stress treatment, the cell membrane permeability increased by 149.36 % in TRV2 leaves and by 73.98 % in TRV-BrTCP9 leaves. In contrast, leaves treated with SmCDs exhibited lower increases in permeability, with TRV2 and TRV-BrTCP9 leaves showing increases of 65.76 % and 30.17 %, respectively (Fig. 4B). In addition, silencing BrTCP9 increased the activity of SOD and POD, which were higher than those of the TRV2 under dark stress treatment. SmCDs treatment always increased the activities of SOD, POD and CAT, but the effect on CAT activity was not significant. After 5 d of dark stress treatment, the SOD, POD, and CAT activity of BrTCP9-silenced plants were still 18.08 %, 24.73 %, and 6.41 % higher than those of TRV2 plants, respectively, while those of TRV-BrTCP9 plants treated with SmCDs were 56.79 %, 95.25 % and 13.56 % higher compared with TRV2 plants (Fig. 4C-4E). The above results showed that the ROS production was related to the transcription of BrTCP9 in the process of senescence, while SmCDs could inhibit the transcription of BrTCP9 to keep the homeostasis of ROS metabolism in flowering Chinese cabbage.

Additionally, the expression of Rboh key enzyme genes responsible for catalyzing ROS production in plants was analyzed. Silencing BrTCP9 resulted in the downregulation of BrRbohA and BrRbohD, while the expression levels of BrRbohB and BrRbohF remained largely unaffected compared to control plants. However, in the presence of SmCDs, the transcript level of BrRbohA was further suppressed (Fig. 4F).

The above results suggested that BrTCP9 could increase the accumulation of ROS by inhibiting the activity of key antioxidant enzymes and promoting the expression of pivotal Robh genes, while SmCDs could delay aging by retarding the transcription of BrTCP9 to reduce the accumulation of ROS and maintain the homeostasis of ROS metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BrTCP9 directly targets the BrRbohA promoter through the GGNCCCAC motif
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the involvement of BrTCP9 in the regulatory mechanism of SmCDs-delayed leaf senescence and whether BrTCP9 can directly target the respiratory burst oxidase homologue gene BrRbohA has attracted our attention. Previous reports have shown that TCP transcription factors can specifically recognise GGNCCCAC and GTGGNCCC elements (Sunkara, 2021). Therefore, Cis-acting elements within 2000 bp of the BrRbohA promoter were analyzed using Plantpan 4.0 (http://plantpan.itps.ncku.edu.tw/plantpan4/promoter_results.php) online software, revealing the presence of a GGTCCCAC sequence ( Fig. 5A). Firstly, it was demonstrated by a LUC system that BrTCP9 could activate BrRbohA expression, and the combination of BrTCP9–62SK + BrRbohA-0800 resulted in the strongest fluorescence of the leaves, with the ratio of LUC/REN was much higher than that of the control (Fig. 5B-5C). The results of the Y1H assay indicated that the BrTCP9-AD + BrRbohApro-pAbAi experimental group was able to grow normally on the selective medium SD/-Leu (AbA100), whereas no growth was observed in the control group (AD + BrRbohApro-pAbAi) (Fig. 5D). This suggested that BrTCP9 could interact with the BrRbohA gene. Additionally, the results of the GUS tissue staining experiments showed that co-transformation of Pro35S: BrTCP9 and ProRbohA: GUS into tobacco leaves resulted in blue staining of the leaves. In contrast, leaves transformed with ProRbohA: GUS or Pro35S: GUS alone (control) remained white (Fig. 5E), indicating that BrTCP9 bound to BrRbohA and activated its transcription.

Furthermore, to further analyze whether BrTCP9 directly bind to BrRbohA promoter in vitro, electrophoretic mobility shift assay (EMSA) was employed. The results revealed that the presence of GST-BrTCP9 protein produced distinct binding bands, with significant shifts in band mobility, confirming that BrTCP9 directly bound to the promoter region of BrRbohA. The addition of a cold probe gradually weakened the binding, and mutation of the biotin-labeled probe sequence resulted in the absence of binding bands (Fig. 5F). Collectively, these findings fully indicated that BrTCP9 specifically recognized and bound to the GGTCCCAC sequence, thereby activating the expression of BrRbohA in postharvest aging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BrRBohA functions in modulating leaf senescence under the influence of SmCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the functions of BrRBohA in regulating the senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage, we generated TRV-BrRBohA plants using the VIGS technology (Fig. S2). After 5 d of dark treatment of isolated leaves, TRV2 leaves exhibited completely yellowing phenotype, while those of TRV-BrRBohA displayed less yellowing symptom compared with TRV2 leaves. Interestingly, SmCDs treatment partially alleviated the yellowing phenomenon of TRV2 and TRV-BrRBohA leaves. Particularly, the leaves maintained greener in TRV-BrRBohA leaves treated with SmCDs than those in TRV-BrRBohA leaves ( Fig. 6A). Compared with the TRV2 leaves, the chlorophyll content of TRV-BrRBohA leaves increased by 154.88 %, while that of TRV2 and TRV-BrRBohA leaves treated with SmCDs increased by 411.16 % and 362.10 %, respectively, which was consistent with the morphological phenotype (Fig. 6B). Additionally, silencing BrRBohA and spraying SmCDs could downregulate the transcripts of chlorophyll degradation-related genes such as BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH (Fig. 6F).

After 5 d of darkness, TRV2 leaves exhibited extensive orange coloration in their chlorophyll fluorescence. In contrast, TRV-BrRbohA leaves and TRV2 leaves treated with SmCDs showed reduced orange coloration, with TRV-BrRbohA leaves treated with SmCDs displaying significantly less orange than TRV2 leaves (Fig. 6C). The Fv/Fm ratios were higher in both TRV-BrRbohA leaves and TRV2 leaves treated with SmCDs compared to TRV2 leaves. Among all treatments, the highest Fv/Fm ratio was observed in TRV-BrRbohA leaves treated with SmCDs (Fig. 6D). Compared to TRV2 leaves, the Fv/Fm ratio in TRV-BrRbohA leaves increased by 53.81 %, while the Fv/Fm ratios of TRV2 and TRV-BrRbohA leaves treated with SmCDs increased by 25.14 % and 165.21 %, respectively (Fig. 6D). These results were consistent with the fluorescence imaging data (Fig. 6C). As shown in Fig. 6E, the NBT and DAB staining results of TRV2 displayed a relatively dark blue and brown color in leaves, respectively, while silencing BrRBohA and spraying SmCDs had weaker blue NBT and lighter brown DAB staining. Notably, compared to TRV-BrRBohA leaves, TRV-BrRBohA leaves treated with SmCDs displayed weaker blue NBT and lighter brown DAB staining (Fig. 6E). The above results fully demonstrated that BrRBohA played a positive regulatory role in the leaf senescence process, and SmCDs treatment could antagonize the expression of BrRBohA and inhibit ROS production, thereby delaying the leaf senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage.

Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs) have a strong antioxidant effect and can promote plant growth and improve abiotic stress resistance (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021, 2023). Our recent report demonstrated that SmCDs effectively hinder chlorophyll degradation, downregulate expressions of senescence marker genes (BrSAG12), and repress the transcriptions of chlorophyll degradation-associated genes including BrNYC1, BrPAO, BrNYE1, BrPPH, BrNOL, and BrRCCR. Additionally, SmCDs were found to enhance the levels of vitamin C, soluble sugars, sucrose, and flavonoids, thereby delaying senescence and preserving the postharvest quality of flowering Chinese cabbage (Kuang et al., 2023). Despite these findings, the precise molecular mechanisms through which SmCDs delay postharvest senescence remain to be elucidated.

The onset of leaf senescence is strongly correlated with ROS homeostasis. The observations that ROS triggers the development of leaf senescence have been verified by an increase in the accumulation of ROS in aging leaves in grape, rapeseed, and Arabidopsis thaliana (Mittler et al., 2011; Shi et al., 2024). Previous researches have affirmed that most ROS generation and signaling during leaf senescence depend on the respiratory burst oxidase homologues enzymes (Rbohs) activity and their encoding gene transcription (Foreman et al., 2003; Marino et al., 2012; Tan et al., 2020). Melatonin delays senescence through inhibiting the transcriptions of Rboh genes including BrRbohB, BrRbohC, BrRbohD, BrRbohD2, and BrRbohE (Feng et al., 2022). Overexpression of BnaWGR1 can upregulate RbohD and RbohF transcription, induce ROS production, and accelerate leaf senescence (Yang et al., 2018). Our previous study indicated that SmCDs can maintain the homeostasis of ROS metabolism by promoting the activities of CAT, POD, SOD, and APX and retarding the transcriptions of BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF, resulting in procrastinating the senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage (Kuang et al., 2023). To date, although Rbohs is closely related to plant senescence, there is still a lack of verification on the function of Rbohs in regulating senescence. To confirm the function of BrRbohs in regulating leaf senescence, the focus was placed on BrRbohA, which exhibited the most significant induction during senescence and was inhibited by SmCDs in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. We observed that silencing BrRbohA and spraying SmCDs could increase the chlorophyll content, inhibit the transcriptions of chlorophyll degradation-related genes such as BrNYC1, BrNYE1, BrNOL, and BrPPH, and reduce the accumulation of H2O2 and O2 •-, indicating that a novel respiratory burst oxidase homolog gene BrRbohA positively regulating leaf senescence was identified while SmCDs could inhibit production of ROS and mitigate the occurrence of membrane damage due to the ROS accumulation induced by BrRbohA during leaf senescence. These findings fully expounded that BrRbohA was related to leaf senescence and SmCDs could regulate its transcript to impact the leaf senescence progression. Additionally, previous studies have shown that plant senescence is related to Rbohs transcript and regulated by WRKY and NAC transcription factors. For example, BrNAC055 directly binds to NAC-binding sequences (NACBS) in the BrRbohB, BrRbohC-like, promoter and activates their activity, which promotes leaf senescence (Fan et al., 2018). BrWRKY55 activates the expressions of RbohD and SAG13 and regulates ROS and SA accumulation to promote leaf senescence (Wang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, the underlying molecular regulating network of TCP transcription factors mediated ROS production induced by BrRbohs to regulate leaf senescence is limited.

The TCP family is a plant-specific group of transcript factors that play crucial roles in regulating plant growth, development, and the senescence process. Previous reports have ascertained that different TCP proteins exhibit distinct roles in modulating plant senescence. For instance, in peach, virus-induced silencing of PpTCP.A2 has been shown to increase ethylene production and induce fruit ripening. In chrysanthemum, heterologous expression of CmTCP14 in Arabidopsis thaliana has been reported to downregulate the transcript of certain cell cycle-associated genes, enhance chlorophyll and carotenoid accumulation in leaves, and delay senescence (Zhang et al., 2017). Similarly, knockout of TCP8, TCP19, and TCP20 accelerates leaf senescence in Arabidopsis thaliana (Danisman et al., 2013). In tomato, SlTCP12, SlTCP15, and SlTCP18 have been implicated in the regulation of fruit ripening. FaTCP11 has been shown to influence ripening-related processes in strawberry. Conversely, overexpression of ApTCP2 from Acer palmatum in Arabidopsis thaliana accelerates leaf senescence, a process that is at least partially mediated through the modulation of JA biosynthesis (Zhu et al., 2021). BrTCP7 promotes MeJA-induced leaf senescence by activating JA biosynthesis and promoting chlorophyll catabolism (Xu et al., 2019). In contrast, BrTCP21 delays leaf senescence, partially by upregulating the expression of the GA biosynthesis gene BrGA20ox3 (Xiao et al., 2019). To date, the research on the regulation of plant senescence by TCP9 still remains to be explored, especially mediates ROS metabolism. In this study, to identify pivot TCP involved in regulating leaf senescence in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage mediated by SmCDs, sixteen TCP transcription factors were analyzed. It was found that BrTCP9 was the most significantly differentially expressed transcript factor and consistent with the leaf senescence progress, suggesting that BrTCP9 might have a positive regulatory role on leaf senescence. This finding demonstrated that the function of BrTCP9 in regulating leaf senescence is similar to that of ApTCP2 and BrTCP7 (Chen and Yang, 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). Interestingly, SmCDs could inhibit BrTCP9 transcription during senescence, implying that SmCDs might delay leaf senescence by blocking BrTCP9 transcription. Furthermore, amino acid sequence analysis of BrTCP9 revealed the inclusion of a conserved TCP structural domain and BrTCP9 is a nuclear-localised transcriptional activator. This is consistent with the findings of other members of TCP transcription factor family such as BrTCP21 and BrTCP7, which are also localized in the nucleus and have transcriptional activation activity (Xu et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019). To unravel whether inhibiting the transcription of BrTCP9 was required for delaying leaf senescence induced by SmCDs, the VIGS system was used to silence BrTCP9 in flowering Chinese cabbage. We interestingly found that silencing BrTCP9 and spraying SmCDs could hindered the chlorophyll decomposition and the transcripts of chlorophyll degradation-associated genes such as BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrPPH, indicating that SmCDs could effectively retard the decomposition of chlorophyll and leaf senescence induced by BrTCP9. To date, the understanding of TCP-mediated ROS metabolism in plant senescence remains limited. This study collectively observed that silencing BrTCP9 and spraying SmCDs could increase activities of SOD and POD, inhibit expressions of BrRbohA and BrRbohD, diminish the accumulation of H2O2 and O2 •−, and decrease cell membrane permeability. These findings fully elucidated that BrTCP9 played a positive regulatory role in the ROS production during leaf senescence, while SmCDs treatment could effectively antagonize the transcripts of BrTCP9 and Rbohs genes, and increase the activities of antioxidant enzymes, leading to the decreased H2O2 and O2 •- accumulation during leaf senescence, thus attenuating the injury of ROS to plant and delaying the leaf senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage. Although both BrTCP9 and BrRbohA are implicated in aging, their interaction and regulatory relationship have not been previously established. This study initially confirmed that BrTCP9 bound to the BrRbohA promoter by specifically recognizing the GGTCCCAC sequence and activated BrRbohA expression, as demonstrated through LUC, Y1H, GUS, and EMSA assays. This finding is consistent with earlier studies that identified class I TCP proteins binding as GGNCCCAC elements by random binding site selection assays and EMSA tests (Kosugi and Ohashi, 2002; Viola et al., 2011). Taken together, these results further confirmed that BrTCP9 could interact directly with BrRbohA to promote senescence, while inhibiting BrTCP9 mediated BrRbohA to reduce ROS production was a reason for delaying senescence induced by SmCDs.

This study validated that BrTCP9 and BrRbohA were involved in the positive regulation of leaf senescence and ROS production in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. BrTCP9 directly bound to BrRbohA promoter by specifically recognising GGTCCCAC sequence and activated its expression. SmCDs could delay leaf senescence by inhibiting the promoting effect of BrTCP9-BrRbohA module on ROS production. The findings contributed to the understanding of a novel molecular regulatory network wherein SmCDs delayed senescence through BrTCP9-mediated ROS metabolism. Additionally, these results offered practical implications for enhancing the postharvest quality of horticultural products.
